description:
You are a script-developer who has been handed a tiny project tree containing a couple of data files.  
Your goal is to create a reproducible checksum manifest and a corresponding verification log so that the integrity of the files can be checked later on.

Project layout that already exists when you start:
• /home/user/project  
  • data/  
    • file1.txt  
    • file2.bin  

What you must deliver
1. Create the new directory  
   /home/user/project/checksums  

2. Inside that directory create a text file named  
   /home/user/project/checksums/sha256sums.txt  

   Format requirements for sha256sums.txt (strict – will be tested byte-for-byte):
   • One line per file.  
   • Each line is:  
     <64-character-hex-digest><two spaces><relative-path-from-project-root>\n  
   • Use Unix line-endings (LF) only.  
   • The two managed files must appear in this exact order:  
       a) data/file1.txt  
       b) data/file2.bin  

   Example of the required pattern (hashes are examples only – you must compute the real ones):
   ffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffff  data/file1.txt
   eeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeee  data/file2.bin

3. Still inside /home/user/project/checksums create a log file named  
   /home/user/project/checksums/verification.log  

   How to produce the log:
   • From /home/user/project run the sha256sum verification command against sha256sums.txt.  
   • Capture ONLY the two “OK / FAILED” status lines that the command prints for each file, in the same order produced by the tool, into verification.log.  
   • No extra blank lines and no summary lines are allowed in the log – just the two status lines, each terminated with a single LF.  

   Required final content pattern (again, the words “OK” must really appear if the digests match):
   data/file1.txt: OK
   data/file2.bin: OK

4. Do not move, delete or modify the original data files.  
5. Do not add any other files to /home/user/project/checksums.  
6. Make sure every file and directory you create is writable by the current user (no root needed).  

Verification the grader will perform
a) sha256sums.txt exists at the exact path with the exact two lines and correct hashes.  
b) verification.log exists with exactly the two “: OK” lines and nothing else.  
c) Running “sha256sum -c checksums/sha256sums.txt” from /home/user/project reproduces the same two OK lines and zero failures.  

Hints
• Rely on the standard “sha256sum” tool that is pre-installed in all mainstream Linux distributions.  
• Be careful about the double-space separator; many editors collapse whitespace visually.  
• The log file is easiest to obtain by running the verification command and redirecting its stdout to the file, then trimming anything unwanted if the tool emits extra lines in your environment.
truth:
Initial state (pre-populated before the agent starts)
Directory /home/user/project/data already exists with two files:

1. /home/user/project/data/file1.txt  
   Contents: exactly the three ASCII characters “abc” with no terminating newline.  
   SHA-256: ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad

2. /home/user/project/data/file2.bin  
   Contents: exactly the ASCII sentence  
   “The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog”  
   (43 characters, no newline).  
   SHA-256: d7a8fbb307d7809469ca9abcb0082e4f8d5651e46d3cdb762d02d0bf37c9e592

Expected artefacts after correct task execution

A. /home/user/project/checksums/sha256sums.txt with exactly (two trailing LF line endings):
ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad  data/file1.txt
d7a8fbb307d7809469ca9abcb0082e4f8d5651e46d3cdb762d02d0bf37c9e592  data/file2.bin

B. /home/user/project/checksums/verification.log with exactly:
data/file1.txt: OK
data/file2.bin: OK

Each file/directory created by the agent must be owned by the regular user and be writable (permission mode 644 for files, 755 for directories is acceptable).

The grader will:
1. Confirm sha256sums.txt exists with the two exact lines shown above.  
2. Confirm verification.log exists with the two exact lines shown above.  
3. Run “cd /home/user/project && sha256sum -c checksums/sha256sums.txt” and expect stdout to match
   data/file1.txt: OK
   data/file2.bin: OK
and exit with status 0.
